questions.question: Using uncommon usernames can help reduce the effectiveness of password guessing attacks.
questions.explanation: The notes list “Use uncommon usernames” as a mitigation method.

questions.question: Why can comments in source code be dangerous?
options: They are compiled into CPU instructions
They can be visible publicly and may reveal sensitive information
questions.explanation: The notes warn that public source comments can leak system passwords, API credentials, or processing details.

questions.question: A recommended mitigation is to avoid including sensitive information in source code comments.
questions.explanation: The notes explicitly say not to include sensitive info in comments.

questions.question: A race condition vulnerability occurs when:
options: A system has no audit logs
Operations run simultaneously but should run sequentially
questions.explanation: The notes define race conditions as simultaneous operations that should be sequential, creating a small window to exploit.

questions.question: According to the notes, a race condition is commonly exploited using:
questions.explanation: The notes describe exploiting race conditions via timing attacks to hit a small window when rules/processes aren’t applied.

questions.question: Race conditions are generally high difficulty for attackers and often require significant reconnaissance.
questions.explanation: The notes state race conditions are high difficulty and require high levels of reconnaissance to time correctly.

questions.question: Hidden elements in web pages are risky because:
options: They are always encrypted and cannot be modified
They pass information to the application and can be altered via DOM manipulation
They prevent SQL injection by default
questions.explanation: The notes explain hidden elements are visible in source and can be changed via DOM manipulation, allowing malicious values.

questions.question: Cross-site scripting (XSS) is best described as:
options: Overloading a service to deny availability
Injecting untrusted content that can execute scripts in a victim’s browser
Tricking a user into clicking a hidden UI element
Using DNS requests to retrieve database records
questions.explanation: XSS is an injection flaw where untrusted input is interpreted as executable script/HTML in the browser context.

questions.question: In DOM-based XSS, what is a “sink”?
options: A DOM object/function that allows JavaScript execution or HTML rendering
questions.explanation: The notes define sinks as DOM objects/functions that enable JS execution or HTML rendering.

questions.question: Which of the following is listed as a DOM-based XSS sink?
questions.explanation: The notes list sinks such as eval, setTimeout, setInterval, document.write, and element.innerHTML.

questions.question: A sink is a DOM object or function that can lead to JavaScript execution or HTML rendering.
questions.explanation: That is the definition given in the DOM-based XSS section.

questions.question: The goal of DOM-based XSS (per the notes) is to:
options: Place data into a source so it reaches a sink and triggers script execution
Force the database to generate an error message
questions.explanation: DOM XSS aims to get attacker-controlled data from a source into a sink that executes/renders it.

questions.question: XSS evasion techniques are best described as methods to:
options: Bypass browser or application XSS filters
Combine two SQL queries into one
questions.explanation: The notes define XSS evasion techniques as bypassing XSS filters and validation.

questions.question: Which XSS evasion technique tries to avoid regex detection by representing characters differently (e.g., entity/hex encoding)?
questions.explanation: Encoding characters can evade simplistic regex-based filters.

questions.question: Whitespace embedding as an XSS evasion technique works because:
options: Consecutive whitespace can be ignored by HTML, breaking some regex patterns
HTTP always compresses whitespace to save bandwidth
Browsers refuse to render pages with whitespace
questions.explanation: The notes explain consecutive whitespace can be ignored by HTML and can break regex-based detections.

questions.question: Tag manipulation as an XSS evasion technique refers to:
options: Nesting/manipulating tags to bypass pattern-based filters
questions.explanation: The notes describe tag manipulation (e.g., nesting tags) as a way to evade filters.

questions.question: Built-in browser XSS filters are ineffective against Stored and DOM-based XSS, according to the notes.
questions.explanation: The notes state browser filters are ineffective against Stored and DOM XSS.

questions.question: According to the notes, server-side input validation is effective against which XSS types?
questions.explanation: The notes describe server-side application defenses as effective against Stored and Reflected XSS.

questions.question: Which mitigation rule is: “HTML Escape Before Inserting Untrusted Data into HTML Element Content”?
options: Deny all content unless rules are followed
questions.explanation: This is one of the OWASP-style rules listed in the notes for preventing XSS.

questions.question: Which mitigation approach is recommended before inserting untrusted data into HTML attributes (like id, class, value)?
options: Attribute escape before inserting into HTML common attributes
questions.explanation: The notes list attribute escaping as a key rule for preventing XSS in attribute contexts.

questions.question: Some JavaScript functions can never safely use untrusted data as input, even if that data is JavaScript-escaped.
questions.explanation: This warning is explicitly included in the XSS mitigations section of the notes.

questions.question: Cross-site Request Forgery (CSRF / XSRF) is:
options: A method for bypassing XSS filters using encoding
Unauthorized commands transmitted from a user trusted by the application
A technique to combine multiple SQL queries
questions.explanation: The notes define CSRF as unauthorized commands transmitted from a trusted user (session riding / one-click attacks).

questions.question: CSRF is also known as:
questions.explanation: The notes explicitly mention CSRF is also known as one-click attacks and session riding.

questions.question: CSRF exploits the trust that an application has in a user’s browser.
questions.explanation: That phrasing appears directly in the CSRF section of the notes.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes XSS from CSRF?
options: XSS exploits trust a user has in a site; CSRF exploits trust a site has in a user’s browser
XSS only affects databases; CSRF only affects cookies
XSS requires an authenticated user; CSRF never does
They are the same attack with different names
questions.explanation: The notes frame the difference as “trust user has in a site” (XSS) vs “trust application has in user’s browser” (CSRF).

questions.question: CSRF is commonly accomplished by:
options: Tricking a logged-in user’s browser into sending an unwanted request
Flooding the server with ICMP packets
questions.explanation: The notes describe CSRF as tricking a browser into executing an unwanted action while the user is authenticated.

questions.question: Why does CSRF make it difficult for a server to distinguish a legitimate request from a forged one?
options: Because the user is usually authenticated, so the forged request looks valid
Because it changes the TCP 3-way handshake
Because it always causes a database error
questions.explanation: If the victim is logged in, the browser sends normal authentication context, so the request can look legitimate.

questions.question: Clickjacking is best described as:
options: Forcing a database to respond slowly
Tricking a user into clicking an element that is invisible or disguised
Cracking a password using a common password list
Scanning web servers for open ports
questions.explanation: The notes define clickjacking as tricking a user into clicking a disguised or invisible element.

questions.question: Clickjacking can cause users to unwittingly engage in an attacker’s malicious plan.
questions.explanation: That risk is explicitly listed in the clickjacking section.

questions.question: In SQL injection, the UNION technique is based on:
options: Combining multiple queries using the UNION operator
questions.explanation: UNION-based SQL injection leverages the UNION operator to combine results of multiple SELECT statements.

questions.question: Error-based SQL injection relies on:
options: Database error messages to reveal information
questions.explanation: Error-based techniques force errors to refine/learn information about the database structure.

questions.question: Which database model abbreviation (from the notes) corresponds to a relational database model?
questions.explanation: RDMS refers to a relational database management system/model (relational databases).

questions.question: Time delay SQL injection techniques work by:
options: Making the database wait before responding so timing reveals TRUE/FALSE outcomes
Combining multiple SELECT statements into one response
questions.explanation: Time-based techniques deliberately delay the response; the attacker infers results from response timing.

questions.question: In-band SQL injection means:
options: The attacker uses the same channel to launch the attack and retrieve results
The attacker must use DNS to retrieve results
The attacker can only learn results by response timing
The attacker cannot interact with the database
questions.explanation: In-band SQLi uses the same communication channel for injection and for receiving the data.

questions.question: Out-of-band SQL injection is characterized by:
options: Using the same channel for both attack and results
Using a different channel (e.g., DNS or HTTP requests) to retrieve data
questions.explanation: Out-of-band SQLi retrieves data through another channel like DNS/HTTP requests, rather than the main app response channel.

questions.question: Blind (inferential) SQL injection focuses on reconstructing:
options: The database structure by observing application responses
questions.explanation: Blind SQLi infers database structure via responses (content or timing) instead of directly seeing the data.

questions.question: Which comparison best matches Boolean-based vs Time-based blind SQL injection?
options: Boolean-based relies on content differences; time-based relies on response timing
Boolean-based uses DNS; time-based uses UNION
Boolean-based always returns the full dataset; time-based never runs queries
questions.explanation: Boolean-based observes changes in response content; time-based observes delays in response time.

questions.question: Fingerprinting the database means trying to learn:
options: As much as possible about the DBMS (type, version, metadata)
Only the web server’s open ports
Only the browser’s XSS filter rules
questions.explanation: Fingerprinting gathers information like DBMS type/version/metadata to enable more efficient exploitation.

questions.question: Which mitigation is most directly recommended to prevent SQL injection?
options: Rely only on browser XSS filters
Expose detailed database errors in responses
questions.explanation: The notes list mitigations such as immutable/static queries, parameterized queries, and stored procedures.

questions.question: Stored procedures are listed as one mitigation technique against SQL injection.
questions.explanation: Stored procedures are included in the SQL injection mitigations list in the notes.
